title: One-Shot Heterogeneous Federated Learning with Local Model-Guided Diffusion Models
abstract: In recent years, One-shot Federated Learning (OSFL) methods based on Diffusion Models (DMs) have garnered increasing attention due to their remarkable performance. However, most of these methods require the deployment of foundation models on client devices, which significantly raises the computational requirements and reduces their adaptability to heterogeneous client models. In this paper, we propose FedLMG, a heterogeneous one-shot Federated learning method with Local Model-Guided diffusion models. In our method, clients do not need access to any foundation models but only train and upload their local models, which is consistent with traditional FL methods. On the clients, we employ classification loss and batch normalization loss to capture the broad category features and detailed contextual features of the client distributions. On the server, based on the uploaded client models, we utilize backpropagation to guide the server’s DM in generating synthetic datasets that comply with the client distributions, which are then used to train the aggregated model. By using the local models as a medium to transfer client knowledge, our method significantly reduces the computational requirements on client devices and effectively adapts to scenarios with heterogeneous clients. Extensive quantitation and visualization experiments on three large-scale real-world datasets, along with theoretical analysis, demonstrate that the synthetic datasets generated by FedLMG exhibit comparable quality and diversity to the client datasets, which leads to an aggregated model that outperforms all compared methods and even the performance ceiling, further elucidating the significant potential of utilizing DMs in FL.
impact: Given that our method utilizes a pre-trained diffusion model, there is a possibility of generating sensitive or private information. However, the Stable Diffusion model we rely on has been equipped with a robust safety-checking mechanism designed to minimize such risks. We also conduct sufficient experiments to demonstrate the performance of our method in privacy protection. As a result, we feel that our method does not raise additional significant concerns or potential broader impacts that warrant specific attention or further discussion here.

query_input.scenario: FL clients with heterogeneous model architectures (varying device capabilities)
query_input.issue: Synthetic data overfits to local model biases, causing global model performance drops.
hazard: Synthetic data generated via diffusion from heterogeneous local models in one-shot FL may not faithfully capture the real data distribution, causing the global model to learn from distorted samples.
exposure: End users of the aggregated model in a production service.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: End users depend on the aggregated model and lack transparency or alternatives to detect or counterbalance its degradation.
hipporag_result.impact: Degraded global model performance causes incorrect or biased outputs to end users, undermining service reliability and user trust.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Synthetic data from heterogeneous one-shot FL models distorting the real distribution causes the global model to learn biased patterns, and through end users' unmonitored dependency in production, triggers degraded performance, incorrect outputs, and eroded trust.

dose_response: Synthetic data from heterogeneous local models distorts the real data distribution, causing global model inaccuracies that, when deployed to end users in production, lead to unreliable service and harm.
vulnerability: Divergent local model biases distort synthetic data distribution, impairing global model generalization.
impact: Production model accuracy drops, leading to unreliable service and user harm.
